perturbation.original_text: 10. This Agreement shall be construed, interpreted, and enforced according to the laws of the Commonwealth of Virginia and the parties hereto consent to jurisdiction therein.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Neither party shall disclose the Proprietary Information of the other party to any third party whatsoever by without the prior written approval of the other party and all such information shall be protected with at least the same diligence, care, and precaution that each party uses to protect its own Proprietary Information. 10. This Agreement shall be construed, interpreted, and enforced according to the laws of the Commonwealth of Virginia and the parties hereto consent to jurisdiction therein.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the governing law clause to the middle of the confidentiality clause can obscure it, making it less prominent and potentially impacting its enforceability. This placement is not typical and could be argued as a deliberate attempt to bury the clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the contract specifies Virginia law, 28 U.S. Code § 1404 allows for a change of venue in federal court cases for the convenience of parties and witnesses, and in the interest of justice. Embedding the governing law and jurisdiction clause within the confidentiality section makes it less visible and could complicate arguments for a change of venue based on the aforementioned federal statute.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) perturbation.justification: The structural flaw of embedding the governing law clause within the confidentiality section, as described in the Perturbation Explanation, introduces a legal contradiction. While the original and changed texts both specify Virginia law, the new location of the governing law clause makes it less visible and could complicate arguments for a change of venue based on 28 U.S. Code § 1404, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Both Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 detail 28 U.S. Code § 1404, which allows for a change of venue for the convenience of parties and witnesses. By obscuring the governing law clause, the modified contract undermines the process by which a party could invoke this federal statute, creating a structural flaw that contradicts the law. This constitutes a significant weakening of a legally relevant disclosure, fitting the Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction definition and necessitating a 'YES' assessment.

perturbation.original_text: The undertakings and obligations of the parties under this Agreement shall not apply to any information which: (a) is disclosed in a printed publication available to the general public, is described by or in a patent anywhere in the world, or is otherwise in the public domain at the time of disclosure other than through the wrongful act or omission of the receiving party; (b) is generally disclosed to third parties by the disclosing party without restriction on such third parties; (c) is approved for release by prior, express, written authorization from the disclosing party.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. No modification, alteration, or addition to this Agreement shall be binding unless mutually agreed to in writing by both parties. The undertakings and obligations of the parties under this Agreement shall not apply to any information which: (a) is disclosed in a printed publication available to the general public, is described by or in a patent anywhere in the world, or is otherwise in the public domain at the time of disclosure other than through the wrongful act or omission of the receiving party; (b) is generally disclosed to third parties by the disclosing party without restriction on such third parties; (c) is approved for release by prior, express, written authorization from the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: By placing the exceptions to confidentiality after the clause that deals with modifications, it allows for potentially confusing interpretation. It suggests that the exceptions to confidentiality can be altered with mutual agreement, even if such alterations contradict existing laws regarding publicly available information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act defines trade secrets in a way that generally excludes publicly available information. By implying that the exceptions can be modified, one could interpret that publicly available information can *become* a trade secret via mutual agreement. Such an interpretation contradicts 18 U.S.C. § 1839(3)(A) where it discusses publicly available information as not being a trade secret.
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a structural flaw by placing exceptions to confidentiality after the modification clause. The Law Explanation argues this implies that these exceptions, which include publicly available information, could be modified by mutual agreement. This contradicts the Economic Espionage Act, which excludes publicly available information from being considered a trade secret (as described in the Law Explanation referencing 18 U.S.C. § 1839(3)(A)). Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to the Economic Espionage Act of 1996 and its codification in 18 U.S.C. § 1839, supporting the Law Explanation's claim. By suggesting that publicly available information *could* become a trade secret through modification, the changed text introduces a legally contradictory interpretation as it goes against established law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is entered into this ___ day of July, 2000, between INFe.com, Inc. ("INFE"), and International Data Operations, Inc. ("IDO"), having offices at: 200 Route 17, Mahwah N.J. 07340.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. No modification, alteration, or addition to this Agreement shall be binding unless mutually agreed to in writing by both parties. This Agreement is entered into this ___ day of July, 2000, between INFe.com, Inc. ("INFE"), and International Data Operations, Inc. ("IDO"), having offices at: 200 Route 17, Mahwah N.J. 07340.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the start of the contract, after the modifications section is concerning. Logically it can be implied that the *start* of the contract can be modified, and therefore not binding unless modified and in writing, but also implies that the initial signing of the contract may not be binding, due to modification clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: Basic contract law requires mutual assent (agreement) to form a binding agreement. Placing the clause about modifications *before* the clause identifying the parties and the agreement's effective date creates ambiguity as to whether there was initial mutual assent, potentially conflicting with fundamental contract principles under Virginia Law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Virginia Law LIS Session Information Bills & Resolutions State Budget Virginia Law Reports to the General Assembly Virginia Law All All Code of Virginia Administrative Code Constitution Charters Authorities Compacts Uncodified Acts Code of Virginia The Code of Virginia contains the laws passed by the General Assembly and signed by the Governor for the Commonwealth of Virginia. Updated July 1 to reflect the legislation passed during the previous session. Code of Virginia » Virginia Administrative Code The Virginia Administrative Code contains the regulations adopted by state agencies in the Commonwealth of Virginia. There are 24 titles, each representing a subject category with agencies assigned to the appropriate title. Updated daily. Virginia Administrative Code » Constitution of Virginia The Constitution of Virginia defines and limits the powers of state government and the basic rights of the citizens of the Commonwealth of Virginia. Like all other state constitutions, it is supreme over Virginia's laws and acts of government, though it may be superseded by the United States Constitution and U.S. federal law. Updated annually after legislative session. Constitution of Virginia » Charters Charters are governing documents for counties, cities, and towns in the Commonwealth of Virginia, and may be codified (included in the Code of Virginia) or uncodified. Updated annually. Charters » Authorities Authorities are entities created by the General Assembly to exercise power concerning a limited topic, such as health care or transportation, frequently in a specified region of Virginia. The governing documents for Authorities may be codified (included in the Code of Virginia) or uncodified. Updated annually. Authorities » Compacts A compact is an interstate agreement between two or more signatory states that is approved by Congress. Updated annually. Compacts » Uncodified Acts Uncodified acts are Acts of Assembly that are not part of the Code of Virginia and that are limited in duration of time and effect. These acts have special application relating to particular persons, things, or places. Updated annually. Uncodified Acts of Assembly » Virginia Law Library The Code of Virginia, Constitution of Virginia, Charters, Authorities, Compacts and Uncodified Acts are now available in EPub eBook formats. Helpful Resources Virginia Code Commission Virginia Register of Regulations U.S. Constitution For Developers The Virginia Law website data is available via a web service for developers to access.
perturbation.justification: The Perturbation Explanation states the modification clause, when placed before the contract's commencement details, creates uncertainty regarding the initial agreement's binding nature. The Law Explanation reinforces this, asserting that the placement generates ambiguity about mutual assent, conflicting with fundamental contract principles. The Structural Flaws definition states that the organization of a contract affects its clarity or enforceability, and a legal contradiction arises when a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant or misleading section, making it difficult for parties to locate or interpret. The re-ordering of the contract's start date/parties makes it difficult to understand when the agreement came into effect.
